did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and evaluates a Hybrid Explainable Vision-Aided Deep Learning (HEVADL) framework for skin lesion detection and classification. The method combines Vision Transformers (ViT), Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN), Federated Learning (FL), and Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) for feature selection and optimization. It was tested on dermatology datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2020, DERMOFIT) to achieve high accuracy in skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new algorithm called Hybrid Explainable Vision-Aided Deep Learning (HEVADL). It details the methodology (combining ViT, DCNN, Federated Learning, and ACO), describes the implementation on specific datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2020, DERMOFIT), and reports original quantitative results (e.g., 98.70% accuracy on DERMOFIT) comparing the proposed model against baselines like ResNet50 and Xception. Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is to introduce and validate a new technical solution, classifying it as a research paper.

title: Hybrid Vision Transformers and Federated Learning for Precision and Privacy in Skin Lesion Detection
journal: Proceedings of the Third International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS-2025)
brief_summary: This study proposes the Hybrid Explainable Vision-Aided Deep Learning (HEVADL) framework for skin lesion detection and classification to address challenges like dataset imbalance and lack of transparency. The model integrates Vision Transformers (ViT), Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN), Federated Learning for privacy, and Ant Colony Optimization for feature selection, utilizing Possibilistic Fuzzy C-Means for segmentation. The framework was evaluated on the HAM10000, ISIC 2020, and DERMOFIT datasets using an 80-20 train-test split. HEVADL achieved classification accuracies of 98.22%, 98.46%, and 98.70% on the respective datasets, outperforming baselines such as ResNet50, Xception, and DenseNet201. The results demonstrate a 12% increase in accuracy and a 15% reduction in false negatives compared to existing deep learning models. The research concludes that HEVADL provides a precise, interpretable, and privacy-preserving benchmark for automated skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied across all three datasets. HAM10000 includes melanoma as one of seven classes. ISIC 2020 is labeled for binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). DERMOFIT includes melanoma among its 10 classes.
Studied in the HAM10000 dataset, which contains seven classes including melanocytic nevi.
Studied in the HAM10000 dataset, which contains seven classes including seborrheic keratosis.
Studied in the ISIC 2020 dataset, which is labeled for binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma).
Studied in the DERMOFIT dataset, which comprises 10 classes including basal cell carcinoma.

The paper notes that HAM10000 has seven classes and DERMOFIT has 10 classes, but does not list all specific class names for these datasets beyond melanoma, nevi, seborrheic keratosis, and basal cell carcinoma.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma vs. non-melanoma, or specific classes within HAM10000 and DERMOFIT datasets)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin lesion detection and classification'. It reports classification accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, specificity, and sensitivity on three datasets: HAM10000 (7 classes), ISIC 2020 (binary: melanoma vs. non-melanoma), and DERMOFIT (10 classes). The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions, containing 10,015 images across seven classes (including melanocytic nevi, melanoma, and seborrheic keratosis).

use.training: Used for training the HEVADL model (80% split). Class imbalance addressed using data augmentation and SMOTE.
use.testing: Used for testing the HEVADL model (20% split).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A public dataset aimed at detecting melanoma, containing approximately 33,126 images labeled for binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma).

use.training: Used for training the HEVADL model (80% split). Class imbalance addressed using oversampling and synthetic generation of data.
use.testing: Used for testing the HEVADL model (20% split).

datasets_used.name: DERMOFIT
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising 1,300 dermoscopic images of 10 classes (including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma) with an even class balance.

use.training: Used for training the HEVADL model (80% split).
use.testing: Used for testing the HEVADL model (20% split).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using absolute metric values (accuracy, precision, recall, etc.) and percentage improvements (e.g., 12% increase in accuracy, 15% reduction in false negatives) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.dataset_description: 10,015 dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions of seven classes (melanocytic nevi, melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, etc.). Split: 80% training (8,012 images), 20% testing (2,003 images). Class imbalance addressed using data augmentation and SMOTE.
HAM10000.notes: HEVADL outperformed all baseline models. The confusion matrix highlighted strong classification performance across three classes with minimal misclassifications.

ISIC 2020.dataset_description: Approximately 33,126 images labeled for binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). Split: 80% training (26,500 images), 20% testing (6,626 images). Class imbalance addressed using oversampling and synthetic generation of data.
ISIC 2020.notes: HEVADL showed balanced performance with low false positives and false negatives. The confusion matrix demonstrated the robustness of the model.

DERMOFIT.dataset_description: 1,300 dermoscopic images of 10 classes (including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma) with even class balance. Split: 80% training (1,040 images), 20% testing (260 images).
DERMOFIT.notes: HEVADL achieved the highest accuracy among all datasets (98.70%). The confusion matrix reflected high accuracy across all classes, indicating adaptability even in resource-constrained datasets. DERMOFIT is identified as the best dataset for this study.

Overall Comparative Results.Improvement_vs_Baselines: 12% increase in accuracy and 15% reduction in false negatives compared to existing deep learning models (ResNet50, Xception, DenseNet201, Inception-v3).
Overall Comparative Results.False_Negative_Reduction: 15% reduction in false negatives compared to Xception.
Overall Comparative Results.Training_Efficiency: 20% reduction in training time due to Ant Colony Optimization (ACO).

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning used; Vision Transformer (ViT) and Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) models pre-trained on ImageNet, then fine-tuned on dermatology-specific datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2020, DERMOFIT).
architecture_used.input_size: Resized to a uniform size (exact dimensions not reported); noise eliminated using Dull Razor method.

components: Vision Transformers (ViT) for feature extraction (divides images into small patches to capture long-range relations).
Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNN) for feature extraction (constructs rich features using many layers).
Feature Optimization for better feature selection and enhancement of classification precision.
Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) for feature selection (selects optimum features, removes remaining ones, reduces training time by 20%).
Possibilistic Fuzzy C-Means (PFCM) clustering for image segmentation (identifies skin lesions by dividing region of interest from skin images).
Federated Learning (FL) with FedAvg algorithm for decentralized training and privacy preservation.

training_details.training_method: Federated Learning (FedAvg algorithm used to average model weights trained locally to one global model).
data_augmentation: SMOTE (for HAM10000 and ISIC 2020 datasets to address class imbalance)
Oversampling and synthetic generation of data (for ISIC 2020)

classification_or_output_head.classes: Multi-class (7 classes for HAM10000, 10 classes for DERMOFIT) and Binary (melanoma vs. non-melanoma for ISIC 2020).

preprocessing_techniques: Dull Razor (noise elimination for artifacts like hair)
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) for class imbalance
Oversampling and synthetic generation of data (for ISIC 2020)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Flipping, rotation, and scaling of images were applied to bring diversity to the dataset, particularly for small datasets.
Used to address class imbalance in the HAM10000 and ISIC 2020 datasets.
Applied to the ISIC 2020 dataset to address class imbalance.

conclusions: The proposed Hybrid Explainable Vision-Aided Deep Learning (HEVADL) framework outperformed baseline models (ResNet50, Xception, DenseNet201, and Inception-v3) in skin lesion detection and classification.
HEVADL achieved classification accuracies of 98.22% on HAM10000, 98.46% on ISIC 2020, and 98.70% on DERMOFIT datasets.
The DERMOFIT dataset yielded the highest accuracy (98.70%) among the tested datasets, while HAM10000 yielded the lowest (98.22%).
HEVADL demonstrated an aggregate accuracy of 96.8% across all datasets, representing a 12% increase in accuracy compared to existing deep learning models.
The model achieved a 15% reduction in false negatives compared to Xception, attributed to the use of Possibilistic Fuzzy C-Means (PFCM) segmentation and Ant Colony Optimization (ACO).
The integration of Federated Learning enabled privacy-preserving, decentralized training, maintaining data sensitivity for patients.
The inclusion of Grad-CAM++ provided explainability through heatmaps, increasing clinical utility and trust by highlighting important lesion regions.

Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) reduced training time by 20% while retaining important features, making the model computationally efficient and suitable for deployment on edge devices like mobile health apps.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding generalizability across various skin types and recommends future research to apply the model in actual clinical environments and strengthen robustness for diverse skin tones.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges limitations regarding generalizability across various skin types and recommends future research to apply the model in actual clinical environments and strengthen robustness for diverse skin tones.

The paper relies on publicly available datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2020, DERMOFIT), which may be limited in their ability to represent the full range of skin conditions and types found in real-world clinical settings.

The paper does not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests; performance comparisons were made using absolute metric values and percentage improvements without statistical significance testing.

The DERMOFIT dataset yielded the highest accuracy (98.70%) among the tested datasets, while HAM10000 yielded the lowest (98.22%), indicating potential dataset-specific bias or variability in model performance.
The model was tested using an 80-20 train-test split for all datasets, with no mention of cross-validation or external validation on independent cohorts.
The paper claims a 15% reduction in false negatives compared to Xception, but does not provide confidence intervals or statistical significance for this improvement.
The study addresses class imbalance using data augmentation (flipping, rotation, scaling), SMOTE, and oversampling, but does not detail the potential impact of synthetic data on model generalization.
The paper highlights the use of Federated Learning for privacy preservation, but does not provide empirical evidence of privacy leakage or security metrics in the experimental results.

The paper notes that traditional CNNs experience problems like narrow generalizability and diagnostic accuracies, positioning the proposed HEVADL model as a solution, but does not provide a detailed ablation study isolating the contribution of each component (ViT, DCNN, ACO, PFCM, FL).
